definition: ICT's foundational framework for understanding why markets move. Price is not driven by supply and demand — it is an algorithmically delivered mechanism that harvests retail liquidity to fill institutional orders.
feeds_into: liquidity (understanding WHERE pools are is the core skill)
market_structure (structure exists to BUILD and DELIVER to liquidity pools)
sessions_kill_zones (institutions deliver price at specific scheduled times)
daily_bias (all bias work flows from understanding the institutional draw)
related_to: seasonal_tendencies (macro context layer that frames institutional intent)
IPDA (the algorithmic delivery engine that executes this philosophy)

retail_vs_institutional.the_core_asymmetry: Retail traders trade WITH the crowd. Institutions trade AGAINST the crowd because they need the crowd's stop orders to fill their own large orders.
retail_vs_institutional.retail_behavior: Retail places stops at obvious swing highs (for shorts) and obvious swing lows (for longs). These stops cluster at technical levels that everyone can see on a chart.
retail_vs_institutional.institutional_need: A hedge fund buying 50,000 contracts cannot simply place a market order — there are not enough sellers at one price. They need a WAVE of retail sell orders (triggered stops) to absorb their buy order.
retail_vs_institutional.the_mechanism: Institutions accumulate a position, then drive price TOWARD the retail stop cluster, trigger those stops (creating a wave of market orders on the opposite side), fill their large institutional order against those stops, then reverse price in their intended direction.
retail_vs_institutional.key_insight: Retail IS the liquidity. Retail stops ARE the institutional entry orders. This is not conspiracy — it is the mechanical reality of how large orders get filled in any market.

stop_loss_hunting.definition: The deliberate movement of price to a level where retail stop orders are clustered, triggering those stops to create the order flow needed for institutional entry.
stop_loss_hunting.is_it_random: No. Stop hunts occur at predictable levels: swing highs, swing lows, round numbers, PDH/PDL, session highs/lows, and equal highs/lows. These are all ICT-defined liquidity pools.
stop_loss_hunting.the_sweep_pattern: Price spikes BEYOND the obvious level (taking stops), then immediately reverses. The spike candle is the manipulation phase of PO3. The reversal IS the institutional entry.
stop_loss_hunting.how_to_use: Do not place stops at the obvious level. Recognize the sweep as a SIGNAL that institutional entry is occurring. Enter WITH institutions after the sweep, not before it.
stop_loss_hunting.common_mistake: Retail sees the spike and panics (or gets stopped out). Informed traders see the spike and prepare to enter in the opposite direction.

draw_on_liquidity.definition: Price always moves TOWARD a liquidity pool — either a BSL (buy stop liquidity above swing highs) or SSL (sell stop liquidity below swing lows). The current draw determines direction.
draw_on_liquidity.the_rule: Price moves TO liquidity, not away from it. At any given moment, there is always a defined draw — the next significant pool of resting orders.
draw_on_liquidity.identifying_the_draw: Look for: equal highs or lows, prior session highs/lows, weekly/monthly opens, round numbers, and overnight gaps. The nearest significant pool on the HTF is the draw.
draw_on_liquidity.hierarchy: Monthly liquidity > Weekly liquidity > Daily liquidity > Session liquidity > Intraday liquidity. Higher timeframe draws override lower timeframe ones.
draw_on_liquidity.trading_application: Once the draw is identified, only take trades that go IN THE DIRECTION of the draw. Counter-draw trades are low probability.

IPDA_framework.definition: Interbank Price Delivery Algorithm — ICT's model for the algorithmic engine that delivers price. Markets are not random; they operate on a programmed delivery mechanism.
IPDA_framework.IPDA_lookback: The algorithm references the prior 20, 40, and 60 trading days to identify where liquidity pools reside. These are the 'reference points' the algorithm uses to target price.
IPDA_framework.time_and_price: The algorithm does not just target price levels — it targets PRICE at SPECIFIC TIMES. This is why kill zones exist. The algorithm is scheduled to deliver price to certain zones during certain session windows.
IPDA_framework.quarterly_shift: Every 13 weeks (quarter), the IPDA shifts reference points. The first 2 weeks of a new quarter often see the prior quarter's trend reversed as the algorithm resets.
IPDA_framework.implication: Trading is not about reading supply and demand — it is about reading the ALGORITHM: where is it going (the draw), when will it get there (kill zones), and what manipulation must occur first (PO3 manipulation phase).

why_retail_loses.primary_reason: Retail places stops at the exact levels institutions need to fill orders. By using textbook technical analysis, retail telegraphs their stop placement to the algorithm.
why_retail_loses.secondary_reason: Retail trades INTO liquidity pools (buying breakouts at highs, selling breakdowns at lows) — the exact moment institutions are exiting, not entering.
why_retail_loses.tertiary_reason: Retail uses indicators that are all derivatives of price — they confirm what already happened, not what is about to happen.
why_retail_loses.the_90_percent_rule: 90% of retail traders lose because they are the product, not the participant. Their stop orders are the raw material that institutions require to conduct business.
why_retail_loses.the_fix: Stop placing stops at obvious levels. Stop buying breakouts. Stop selling breakdowns. Instead: identify the draw, wait for the sweep (manipulation), then enter in the direction of the draw with stops beyond the sweep extreme.

kill_zone_scheduling.definition: The algorithm delivers price at specific, recurring time windows. These are not arbitrary — they correspond to institutional trading desk opening hours globally.

windows.Asian: 8 PM - midnight ET: Accumulation phase. Low volatility range building.
windows.London: 2 AM - 5 AM ET: First major manipulation. London traders hunt stops from Asian session.
windows.NY_AM: 7 AM - 10 AM ET: Highest conviction window. NY open drives the real directional move.
windows.NY_PM: 1:30 PM - 4 PM ET: Secondary distribution or reversal window.
windows.CBDR: 2 PM - 8 PM ET: Algorithm resets. Sets reference range for next 24-hour cycle.

kill_zone_scheduling.key_rule: The manipulation (sweep) almost always occurs DURING a kill zone. Entries made during kill zones have statistically higher success rates.

core_trading_rules.rule_1: Trade FROM liquidity, not INTO it. Enter AFTER a liquidity sweep, not before. Buying into resistance or selling into support means trading against institutional exits.
core_trading_rules.rule_2: The draw determines direction. Never take a trade without first identifying the HTF draw on liquidity.
core_trading_rules.rule_3: Manipulation precedes distribution. Every real move is preceded by a false move in the opposite direction (the stop hunt). Wait for the false move before entering.
core_trading_rules.rule_4: Time validates price. A perfect setup at the wrong time (outside kill zones) has reduced probability. A valid setup during a kill zone has maximum probability.
core_trading_rules.rule_5: HTF controls. A LTF setup against the HTF draw is a losing trade regardless of how perfect it looks on the lower timeframe.
